TALK 4

REPRESENTATION OF INTUITIVE PHYSICS PROPERTIES

Peter Tse1, Sebastian Frank2, Eric Reavis3, Mark Greenlee2;                 INSIGHTS ABOUT FEATURE BINDING FROM

INTERPRETING AND ACCOUNTING FOR INDIVIDUAL
DIFFERENCES IN COLOR VISION
Michael Webster1, Kara Emery2, Camilla Simoncelli1; 1University of
Nevada, Reno, 2New York University

The concept of a “standard” or average observer is central to color
science and application, but masks the marked variability even among
observers with “typical” trichromatic color vision. These differences
arise independently at many levels and affect diverse aspects of color
coding, from genetics and sensitivity to color perception and cognition.
As a result, two observers viewing the same stimulus may have very
different experiences. Measurements of these variations have
provided important and often surprising clues about the mechanisms
and representational structure of color vision. Differences can be
amplified in modern wide-gamut devices, and there is increasing
recognition and interest in accounting for them to better display and
communicate information about color. One approach is to introduce

TALK 3, 8:45 AM, 31.13                                                          The central-peripheral dichotomy (CPD) theory is motivated by the fact
                                                                                that an attentional bottleneck allows only a tiny fraction of visual inputs
WHOLE-BRAIN REPRESENTATION OF OBJECT,                                           to reach recognition. It proposes that the peripheral visual field
ATTENTION, AND LOAD                                                             primarily serves to select this fraction --- by directing gaze or attention

University                                                                      the selected inputs. Because of this bottleneck, the feedforward

Moderator: Joan Ongchoco, University of British Columbia                     The memorability effect refers to the phenomenon that adults reliably
                                                                             remember and forget the same visual stimuli as each other. This adult-
TALK 1, 10:45 AM, 52.11                                                      like consistency emerges in childhood: 4-year-olds show adult-like
                                                                             memory patterns, whereas 3-year-olds remember the same stimuli
                                                                             within age but differ from adults (Guo & Bainbridge, 2024). This study
                                                                             tests whether neural development—particularly within the


91 |   Vis ion Sc ienc es          Society
hippocampus—drives the emergence of adult-like visual memory by             generally slower and relied on fewer items in memory, yet greater

                                                                               Biological motion perception (BMP) refers to humans’ ability to
UNCERTAINTY-WEIGHTED INTEGRATION OF MOTION                                     perceive and recognize the actions of living beings purely from their
AND POSITION: PSYCHOPHYSICAL AND FMRI EVIDENCE                                 motion patterns, even when those actions are presented only as
FROM THE DOUBLE-DRIFT ILLUSION                                                 sparse point-light displays. Neurophysiological studies indicate that
Ke Yin1 (yink23@mail2.sysu.edu.cn), Shijia Zhang2, Jiyan Zou3, Ce              BMP in the human brain arises from coordinated processing across
Mo1; 1Sun Yat-sen University, 2South China Normal University, 3Fudan           the ventral and dorsal visual streams. The seminal two-stream model
University                                                                     of Giese & Poggio (2003) formalized this division of labor, proposing
                                                                               parallel form and motion pathways whose dynamic integration
Visual information from multiple physical dimensions (e.g., motion,            supports robust action recognition. However, this model lacks
position) is integrated to form coherent percepts, yet this process is         recurrent connections and top-down mechanisms, which are
complicated by sensory uncertainty that is ubiquitous in complex visual        supported by neurophysiological evidence but absent from both
scenes. Normative Bayesian accounts propose that information with              classical theories and modern AI systems. Building on this foundation,
low uncertainty should be assigned greater weights during integration,         we introduce the Two-Stream Motion Perceiver (TSMP), which
but direct behavioral and neural evidence for such uncertainty-weight          translates the core Giese & Poggio architecture into a modern deep
integration in vision remains scarce. Here, we leveraged the double-           learning framework while incorporating biologically motivated
drift illusion (DDI) to test how the visual system integrates motion and       recurrent connections and top-down modulations from memories.
position signals with different levels of uncertainty. In this illusion, the   TSMP preserves the separation of form and motion pathways, each

The other-race categorization advantage (ORCA) is a well-established
                                                                            consistently leads to decreased accuracy in detecting perceptual
effect where individuals are faster and better at categorizing other-race

evidence nor RSA can serve as general methodological principles in
vision science. Valid cross-method inference requires explicit forward     PAVILION
models specifying how hypothesized neural representations, noise,
                                                                           26.471 THE CROSS-MODALITY TRANSFER OF
and measurement processes jointly generate empirical observations.

[224655/Z/21/Z].                                                            Accurate perception of positions in depth is essential for human

Functional Organization of Visual                                           depth. Neural selectivity for binocular disparity is distributed across the
Pathways: Neuroimaging                                                      visual cortex in regions as early as V1, though some prior fMRI studies

                                                                            From a control-theoretic perspective, smooth pursuit is an inherently
36.416 A POPULATION CODING APPROACH TO
                                                                            unstable tracking problem: even small temporal errors accumulate
ESTIMATING INTER-OBSERVER CONSISTENCY IN EYE                                until the continuous controller fails and generates a discrete corrective
FIXATIONS                                                                   reset. Adaptive catch-up and backtracking saccades are therefore

Scene Perception: Categorization,                                               Real-world statistical regularities, learned through a lifetime of
                                                                                exposure to structured environments, allow the visual system to form
memory                                                                          predictions about incoming stimuli. Under predictive coding models,
                                                                                statistically regular images generate smaller prediction errors and
TUESDAY, MAY 19, 8:30 AM – 12:30 PM,                                            should therefore be processed more efficiently than statistically
                                                                                irregular images. One important form of such regularity is scene
BANYAN BREEZEWAY                                                                representativeness, where highly representative “good” exemplars are
                                                                                detected more efficiently than “bad” exemplars (Caddigan et al., 2017).

intermittently in either visual hemifield, and participants reported their
                                                                             EY028163 to S. Ling and supported by F99NS124144 and

                                                                             K00EY036804 to L.D. Ramirez.
enhanced visual discrimination accuracy when directing auditory
attention to the speaker located at the same hemifield, compared to
when attending to the opposite location (pbonf = .008) or to a non-
spatial voice cue (pbonf = .048). Exploratory analyses of eye-tracking
data revealed small but systematic gaze shifts toward the attended
hemifield, which did not explain the observed effect of auditory spatial
attention on visual task performance. These findings demonstrate that
sustained auditory spatial attention selectively enhances visual
processing at corresponding locations, revealing robust cross-modal
attentional facilitation under prolonged attentional demands.

56.472 EMOTIONAL AROUSAL ALTERS POPULATION
SPATIAL FREQUENCY TUNING
Luis D. Ramirez1,2, Jasmine Pan1, Janneke F. M. Jehee3, Joseph T.
McGuire1, Sam Ling1; 1Boston University, 2University of California San
Diego, 3Donders Institute for Brain, Cognition, & Behavior, Radboud
University

Emotions guide perception, influencing threat and opportunity
recognition in daily life. Because spatial frequency (SF) processing
defines the scale of detail encoded by the visual system, emotional
modulation of SF sensitivity could have a substantial impact on daily
vision. Indeed, psychophysical work has suggested that negative
emotions may augment SF processing. However, the mechanisms by
which emotion affects visuocortical SF tuning remains unknown. To
address this, we used fMRI to measure voxel-wise population SF
tuning (pSFT) in early visual cortex (V1–V3), while participants heard
sounds that contained either Negative or Neutral valence. To measure
pSFT, we presented bandpass-filtered noise stimuli (0.5–12 cpd; 40
log-spaced steps; 80% contrast; 40-s blocks). During each pSFT
block, participants also heard either Negative or Neutral valence daisy-
chained auditory clips (IADS database), followed by a 2-s arousal
rating window. In a separate baseline condition meant to map SF
preferences in the absence of sounds, participants performed a silent
luminance change detection task at fixation during pSFT mapping.
Importantly, visual statistics were identical across blocks, and the only
difference was what participants heard and the task performed.
Negative emotion was validated by behavioral ratings, pupil dilation,
and amygdala activation. Interestingly, we discovered that Negative
sounds induced a systematic increase in preferred SF, both as a
function of eccentricity, as well as a function of baseline pSFT peak.
Furthermore, this shift was accompanied by increased selectivity
(narrower population-level bandwidths), particularly in extrastriate
